index.hero-h1:
Free Tree Care Tools
by a Certified Arborist
index.hero-sub:
From early pest detection to pruning timing and field surveys.
Professional arboricultural knowledge, freely available online.
index.pro-desc: Tools for certified arborists, landscape architects, and municipal officers — based on ISA and CTLA international standards.
index.pwa-msg: 📲 Add to Home Screen for offline access
index.tool1-desc: Select species, season, and symptoms to instantly identify likely pests/diseases and initial response steps.
index.tool2-desc: Select species and pruning goal to see a color-coded monthly calendar with timing guidance and cautions.
index.tool3-desc: A 32-item field inspection sheet. Record root, trunk, branch, and foliage conditions for an automatic health score.
index.pro1-desc: Based on ISA TRAQ Level II. Evaluates likelihood of failure, impact, and consequences via the ISA risk matrix.
index.pro2-desc: Based on CTLA Guide for Plant Appraisal 10th ed. Useful for transplant compensation, insurance, and litigation.
index.pro3-desc: Record multiple trees consecutively on one site. Auto-generates a sortable risk report. Offline-capable.
index.footer-copy: © 2025 Arborist Tools Japan — All tools are free to use.

diagnosis.title: Pest & Disease Risk Diagnosis | Arborist Tools Japan
diagnosis.sub:
Supervised by a certified arborist. Select symptoms to instantly identify
likely pests/diseases and recommended initial responses.
diagnosis.q2desc: Select the season when symptoms were first noticed
diagnosis.q4desc: Select the environment where the tree is located
diagnosis.ctaDesc:
This tool is for initial screening only. For accurate diagnosis and treatment planning,
an on-site inspection by a certified arborist is recommended.
diagnosis.footer1: This tool is for general information only and is not a substitute for professional diagnosis.

pruning.title: Pruning Season Calendar | Arborist Tools Japan
pruning.sub:
Select species and pruning goal to see a monthly color-coded calendar
with timing guidance — based on professional arboricultural standards.
pruning.ctaDesc:
Heavy pruning of large trees carries significant risk. Incorrect timing or technique
can lead to tree death. Consult a certified arborist or tree care professional.

checklist.title: Tree Health Checklist | Arborist Tools Japan
checklist.sub:
A field inspection sheet. Record root, trunk, branch, and foliage conditions
to automatically calculate a health score and action priority list.
checklist.ctaDesc:
Use this as a field record or arborist report template.
For detailed diagnosis and treatment plans, consult a certified arborist.
checklist.footer1: This tool is for general information only and is not a substitute for professional diagnosis.
checklist.rankDescA: No significant issues detected. Continue regular observation and preventive management.
checklist.rankDescB: Minor issues observed. Early action recommended to maintain tree health.
checklist.rankDescC: Multiple issues identified. Prompt action and arborist consultation recommended.
checklist.rankDescD: Serious issues detected. Immediate risk assessment and urgent action required.
checklist.pAllClear: All items passed. No immediate concerns.
checks: No abnormal swelling or heaving at root collar
Foundation of root system stability. Deformation at ground level may indicate root rot or windthrow risk.
No voids or subsidence in soil around root zone
Signs of underground root decay or soil loss. Probe by stepping gently to confirm.
Surface roots spread radially in all directions
If roots concentrate in one direction, absence of roots on the opposite side may indicate root system deficiency.
Adequate soil space exists around root zone
Root zone diameter should be at least 1/3 of tree height. Check for restrictions from pavement or structures.
No fungal fruiting bodies at base
Presence of Armillaria or similar fungi strongly indicates root decay — a serious hazard warning sign.
Soil is not excessively compacted
High soil compaction reduces aeration and drainage, inhibiting fine root development.
No evidence of herbicide or chemical spray near roots
Chemical infiltration into the root zone can cause serious damage to the root system.
Appropriate mulching (organic material) present at base
Mulching improves soil moisture retention, temperature buffering, and microbial activity.
No significant cavities or decay in trunk
Check for hollow sounds by tapping with a mallet. Cavities are a major risk factor for failure.
No fungal fruiting bodies on trunk
Bracket fungi (e.g., Ganoderma) are evidence of internal decay. High failure risk above infection site.
No severe bark cracking or peeling
Natural bark shedding is normal, but deep longitudinal cracks may indicate frost damage or decay.
No abnormal resin or sap oozing from trunk
Heavy resin flow may indicate boring insect attack (e.g., longhorn beetles) or disease.
Trunk lean within acceptable range (≤15° from vertical)
Excessive lean indicates shifted center of gravity and increased failure risk. Assess with root condition.
No advancing decay at old pruning wounds or injury sites
Check progression of decay at old wounds. Confirm proper callus (wound wood) formation.
No constricting materials (wire, rope, etc.) on trunk
Wire cutting into bark severs vascular tissue, causing tree decline and death.
No basal decay (white rot or brown rot) near ground level
Basal decay severely reduces structural support. Use probe to assess depth when possible.
No deadwood or hanging branches present
Dead branches are prone to decay and can fall during wind or snow. Immediate removal required in occupied areas.
No splits or cracks (codominant stems, included bark)
Codominant stems with included bark are prone to sudden splitting and are high-risk structural defects.
Crown is evenly developed with no major asymmetry
A heavily one-sided crown shifts the center of gravity and increases toppling risk.
Adequate light penetration into the crown interior
Dense crowns become breeding grounds for pests and diseases. Thinning improves air and light.
Previous year's growth within normal range (no severe dieback)
If new shoot growth is less than 50% of normal, it indicates significant decline in tree vigor.
No progressive branch dieback or canker present
Dieback spreading from specific branches may indicate canker disease, decay fungi, or boring insects.
Support stakes are functioning properly without injuring tree
Check that stake ties are not pressing on or damaging bark. Check for looseness or decay.
No hazardous contact with structures, power lines, or fences
Contact with buildings, power lines, or fences poses risk of damage. Regular inspection required.
Leaf color and size are typical for the species
Chlorosis, small leaves, or leaf reduction are typical signs of nutritional or root system disorders.
No premature or excessive leaf drop out of season
Summer leaf drop indicates serious water stress, root damage, or disease. Prompt investigation needed.
No significant leaf spots, lesions, or discoloration
Leaf abnormalities may indicate fungal, bacterial, or viral disease, especially if observed on multiple branches.
No significant feeding damage (holes, skeletonizing, bleaching)
Damage by sawflies, leaf miners, or mites. Heavy infestations can significantly reduce tree vigor.
Leaf density is adequate (no severe thinning of foliage)
If leaf density is reduced in more than 30% of the crown, it indicates significant decline in tree vigor.
No powdery mildew or sooty mold on leaves
Powdery mildew inhibits photosynthesis. Sooty mold grows on honeydew from scale insects or aphids.
No significant infestation by caterpillars, scale, or aphids
Check leaf undersides, new shoots, and branch forks. Avoid bare-hand contact with urticating caterpillars.
Minimal air pollution or salt damage visible
Trees near roads (exhaust, de-icing salt) or coasts (salt spray) are prone to leaf scorch and dieback.

traq.page-title: Tree Risk Assessment (ISA TRAQ) | Arborist Tools Japan
traq.page-label: Professional Tool — ISA TRAQ Level II
traq.sub:
A professional tree risk assessment tool based on ISA Tree Risk Assessment Qualification (TRAQ) Level II.
Results are rated using the ISA standard risk matrix.
traq.isa-badge: 🌐 ISA TRAQ Level II | ANSI A300 Part 9 Compliant
traq.ph-loc: e.g. Main gate, XX Park, along footpath
traq.ph-notes: Record site observations and notable findings here
traq.p1-meta: TRAQ Step 1 — Structural defects, decay, root system
traq.p2-meta: TRAQ Step 2 — Failure trajectory and target proximity
traq.p3-meta: TRAQ Step 3 — Severity of potential damage
traq.p4-meta: TRAQ Step 4 — Recommended actions and urgency
traq.r1-desc: Assess presence and severity of cavities, decay, cracks, codominant stems, and included bark.
traq.r2-desc: Evaluate exposed root condition, basal decay, soil compaction, root zone restrictions, fungal presence, and lean.
traq.r3-desc: Assess deadwood, hanging branches, crown weight imbalance, asymmetry, and branch failure risk.
traq.r4-desc: Evaluate foliage density, new shoot growth, leaf color, and bark condition for overall vitality.
traq.r5-desc: Select the target most likely to be struck if a failure occurs.
traq.r6-desc: Assess the proportion of time people or objects occupy the target zone.
traq.r7-desc: Evaluate the distance from the failure zone to the target and likelihood of impact.
traq.r8-desc: Assess the maximum scale of injury or damage if failure occurs.
traq.r9-desc: Assess the ecosystem services, aesthetic value, and replacement cost if the tree is removed.
traq.r10-desc: Evaluate existing support systems, cabling, bracing, and maintenance history.
traq.cta-desc: Use this result as a formal report, deliverable, or submission to authorities.
traq.footer1: This tool references ISA TRAQ Level II for professional use. It is not a substitute for a legally binding assessment report.

ctla.page-title: Tree Appraisal (CTLA Method) | Arborist Tools Japan
ctla.sub:
A tree monetary value calculator based on the CTLA "Guide for Plant Appraisal" 10th edition.
Useful for transplant compensation, insurance appraisal, litigation support, and development negotiation.
ctla.ctla-badge: 💰 CTLA Guide for Plant Appraisal 10th ed. Compliant
ctla.dbh-desc: Measure trunk diameter at 1.3m above ground. For multi-stem trees, calculate from combined cross-sectional area.
ctla.unit-desc: In Japan, ¥3,000–8,000/cm² is commonly used (ref. MLIT construction cost standards).
ctla.species-desc: Evaluate the species' suitability, rarity, and replacement difficulty for this site (CTLA §6.2).
species-opts: 100% — Optimal species (best suited for this site)
ctla.rarity-desc: Add for heritage trees, notable specimens, or trees with cultural/historical significance.
ctla.condition-desc: Assess current health status. Consider pests, decay, lean, and vigor comprehensively (CTLA §6.3).
ctla.contribution-desc: Importance of this tree to the overall landscape or function (CTLA §6.4.1)
ctla.placement-desc: Land use zone where the tree is located (CTLA §6.4.2)
ctla.site-desc: Overall quality of site conditions: soil, light, moisture, root space (CTLA §6.4.3)
ctla.lc-formula: Tree Value = Basic Value (Area × Unit Price) × Species Rating × Condition × Location Rating
ctla.cta-desc: Use this result for transplant compensation negotiation, insurance appraisal, or regulatory submissions.
ctla.footer1: © 2025 Arborist Tools Japan — CTLA Method Tree Appraisal
ctla.bd-location: Location Rating (avg. of Contribution, Placement, Site)

survey.page-title: Field Survey — Multi-Tree Recording | Arborist Tools Japan
survey.sub:
Record multiple trees consecutively on site and auto-generate a survey report.
PWA & offline capable — works without internet in the field.
survey.ph-location: e.g. XX Park / YY Elementary School / ZZ Building grounds
survey.empty-msg:
Press "＋ Add Tree" to begin
your field survey.
survey.footer1: Data is stored in this browser only. Nothing is sent to a server.
survey.footer2: © 2025 Arborist Tools Japan — Field Survey Tool
survey.card-ph-notes: Observations, photo numbers, and notable findings
survey.report-disclaimer-txt: This report was generated using Arborist Tools Japan (raitopapa.github.io). This tool is for general informational purposes only and is not a substitute for a legally binding arborist report or certified assessment.
